coredata.dc:title: Optical and electrochemical analysis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from Moosa balbeesiaana peels for advanced supercapacitor applications

coredata.dc:description: The demand for suitable electrode materials for energy storage devices, driven by increasing energy needs and environmental concerns, has led to the investigation of green synthesis methods. In this study, a composite material (rGO@NCQDs) comprising nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) derived from Moosa balbeesiaana peels and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was synthesized via hydrothermal methods to evaluate its photophysical properties and electrochemical performance for supercapacitors applications. Additionally, the electrochemical behavior of rGONCQDs combined with Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) was explored. Characterization techniques including FTIR spectroscopy revealed typical carbon-based material features in rGO-decorated NCQDs, and rGONCQDs@V2O5 composite. SEM analysis illustrated distinctive surface structures (mushroom-shaped for rGO@NCQDs and flowered-shaped for rGONCQDs@V2O5), while XRD confirmed crystalline structures with specific sizes. Photophysical investigations demonstrated significant Solvatochromic shifts and strong solute-solvent interactions in the composites. Electrochemical studies, including cyclic Voltammetry and Galvanostatic measurements, exhibited promising performance metrics. Specifically, rGO@NCQDs demonstrated a specific capacitance of 134.68 Fg−1 with excellent retention over 5000 charge-discharge cycles. In contrast, rGONCQDs@V2O5 exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 562.62 Fg−1 at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1 and exceptional cycle stability (96 % retention over 5000 cycles). These findings highlight the potential of the synthesized composites as efficient electrode materials for supercapacitors, offering enhanced electrochemical performance and stability. The study underscores the importance of green synthesis approaches in developing functional materials for sustainable energy storage applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The demand for suitable electrode materials for energy storage devices, driven by increasing energy needs and environmental concerns, has led to the investigation of green synthesis methods. In this study, a composite material (rGO@NCQDs) comprising nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) derived from Moosa balbeesiaana peels and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was synthesized via hydrothermal methods to evaluate its photophysical properties and electrochemical performance for supercapacitors applications. Additionally, the electrochemical behavior of rGONCQDs combined with Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) was explored.

Characterization techniques including FTIR spectroscopy revealed typical carbon-based material features in rGO-decorated NCQDs, and rGONCQDs@V2O5 composite. SEM analysis illustrated distinctive surface structures (mushroom-shaped for rGO@NCQDs and flowered-shaped for rGONCQDs@V2O5), while XRD confirmed crystalline structures with specific sizes.

Photophysical investigations demonstrated significant Solvatochromic shifts and strong solute-solvent interactions in the composites. Electrochemical studies, including cyclic Voltammetry and Galvanostatic measurements, exhibited promising performance metrics. Specifically, rGO@NCQDs demonstrated a specific capacitance of 134.68 Fg−1 with excellent retention over 5000 charge-discharge cycles. In contrast, rGONCQDs@V2O5 exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 562.62 Fg−1 at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1 and exceptional cycle stability (96 % retention over 5000 cycles).

These findings highlight the potential of the synthesized composites as efficient electrode materials for supercapacitors, offering enhanced electrochemical performance and stability. The study underscores the importance of green synthesis approaches in developing functional materials for sustainable energy storage applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No datasets were generated or analyzed during the current study.

ce:section.ce:para: In the current scenario, the demand for energy storage systems is increasing due to their applications in various appliances and in light of environmental concerns. The challenge lies in developing efficient devices/systems to replace batteries and capacitors, while also mitigating the greenhouse effect. Researchers have been exploring novel synthesis techniques, performance optimization strategies, and potential applications beyond traditional energy storage. Significant progress has been made in the field of electrode material for supercapacitors (SCs), driven by the need for sustainable energy solutions [1-3].

Supercapacitors are being extensively explored as promising energy storage solution and offer several advantages over other traditional energy storage devices, including the ability to charge and discharge rapidly, capability of storing substantial amount of energy, delivering high power density and long life span making them ideal for wide range of applications [4-8]. In the area of supercapacitors, various materials have emerged as leading candidates. These include graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [9-11], MXenes [12-14], carbon composites [15], metal organic frameworks [16-17] and conducting polymers [18]. These materials have been instrumental in creating high performance devices with outstanding rate capabilities. With the increasing sustainable energy solutions, ongoing research and innovation in these areas are crucial for advancement of next generation energy storage technologies. Moreover studies have investigated the use of biodegradable waste materials for energy storage applications, further contributing to the development of sustainable energy solutions [19-22].

The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using biodegradable materials to fabricate electrodes for supercapacitors is a prominent area of research [23-27]. There is extensive literature available regarding the synthesis, characterization, and applications of CQDs [28-31], as well as methods to control the size, morphology, and phase of the materials using the hydrothermal method [32]. Moreover, CQDs exhibit various desirable properties such as sensing heavy metals, high chemical stability, photocatalysis, tunable luminescence emission, ultra-compact size, versatile surface, superior electron transfer ability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, making them suitable for applications in energy storage or conversion, biological imaging, catalysis, drug delivery, environmental monitoring, and remediation [33-38]. The addition of nitrogen and sulfur sources such as urea, ethylenediamine, diethanolamine, and thiourea enhances the photoluminescent properties of CQDs, expanding their potential uses [39,40]. Furthermore, the addition of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) to CQDs enhances surface area, conductivity, chemical and thermal properties, and stability [41]. Literature also supports the enhanced energy density of supercapacitors achieved through the addition of carbon-based materials like activated carbon, graphite, carbon nanotubes, and graphene [42], as well as the use of transition metal oxides [43-48], which exhibit high specific capacitance and energy density, making them suitable for supercapacitors.

Green synthesis methods have garnered considerable attention due to their environmental benefits, depleting non-renewable resources and cost effectiveness techniques such as hydrothermal synthesis, Solvothermal methods and the use of biomass derived precursors are being increasingly adopted. With the advancement of green technologies, design and manufacturing of high performance of supercapacitors involving biodegradable materials for energy storage is enticing. For instance, the use of natural waste materials, such as fruits peels flower, leaves and plants extracts [49-51] for synthesis carbon based has shown promising results in producing high performance electrode material with minimal environmental impact. These biomass based precursors contain various electroactive groups such as –COOH, -OH, and NH2. Drying pyrolysis, these groups decompose into elements like oxygen, nitrogen, and sulphur, which then become doped into the carbon lattice [52]. These super capacitors are environmentally friendly and have a long life time. These are expected to grow in coming years as the world is looking for ways to address energy shortages and global warming [53]

Looking ahead to future demand, exploring bio-waste-based carbon materials for fabricating supercapacitor electrodes is emerging as a promising area of research. These materials offer environmentally safe properties, are readily available, cost-effective, and serve as an alternative to fossil fuels. Extensive literature also supports the use of biomass-derived activated carbon in exploring photophysical properties for applications in fluorescence sensors, biomedical imaging, and optoelectronics [54-56], as well as in evaluating supercapacitor electrode material performance [57-70].

However, there remains a gap in the literature regarding the sustainable synthesis of rGO- decorated nitrogen doped CQDs using abundant and low cost biomass sources. The integration of rGO with NCQDs synthesized from renewable sources has not been extensively explored. To address this research focuses on synthesizing rGO-decorated NCQDs using Moosa balbeesiaana peels, an abundant and underutilized agricultural waste from Himalayan region. By leveraging the chemical composition of Moosa balbeesiaana peels, we aim to develop a green synthesis route that not only reduces waste but also produces NCQDs with enhanced properties as a result of nitrogen doping and rGO decoration.

The present work provides a novel, eco friendly synthesis of advanced nanocomposites using Moosa balbeesiaana peels, which find the way for new applications in energy storage, catalysis and environmental remediation. Specifically, we analyze the photophysical properties and ground to excited state dipole moment of the synthesized rGO-decorated NCQDs using Solvatochromic shift method. We conduct a comparative electrochemical analysis of fabricating electrodes to assess enhanced supercapacitor performance rGO@NCQDs with vanadium pentoxide (V2O5). Thus exploring bio- waste based carbon material offering a sustainable and cost effective alternative to traditional materials.

All chemicals and solvents of spectroscopic grade were employed without further purification during experiments. The materials included ethanol, ethylenediamine, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), carbon black, N-methyl-2pyrrolidone (NMP), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), potassium chloride (KCl), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), acetone, and double-distilled water

The fresh fruit of Moosa balbeesiaana was harvested from a local garden in Dehradun, Uttarakhand, India. Initially, the fruit was washed with tap water to remove dust particles and then rinsed with double-distilled water. The cleaned fruit pieces were dried at a constant temperature of 45 °C in an incubator to maintain a dust-free environment.

For the extraction process, 5 gs of fruit peels were mixed with 60 mL of double-distilled water and 10 mL of ethanol in an autoclave. The mixture was heated at 150 °C for 4 h [71,72]. Afterward, 5 mL of the filtered extract was combined with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and 1.5 mL of ethylenediamine to produce nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) embedded in rGO (rGO@NCQDs). The solution was again taken in autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 10 h. The separation of small particles and residue was confirmed by centrifugation at 4500 rotation per minute for approximately 45 min. The small particles were subsequently isolated via filtration using micro-level filter paper. The resulting NCQDs and residue were dried separately in glass Petri dishes at 80 °C.

Additionally, a composite consisting of vanadium pentoxide (V2O5), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and residue of NCQDs was prepared by mixing these materials in an 80:10:10 ratio. The mixture was then placed in an autoclave with 20 mL of double-distilled water and heated at 180 °C in a furnace for 16 h.

The purpose of using ethanol is to dissolve a wide range of organic compounds, making it suitable for extracting precursor materials. Studies show that it effectively dissolves carbon- containing compounds and nitrogen sources, indicating the presence of –OH and –NH functional groups. These groups significantly enhance electrochemical properties such as higher specific capacitance, additional redox-active sites, excellent cycling performance, and long-term stability of the synthesized composites [73,74]. Further, the selection of specific proportions of 80:10:10 for V2O5, rGO, and NCQDs, along with the higher temperature and extended time in the final step, were considered to ensure the formation of a stable composite structure with V2O5. This ratio optimizes the pseudocapacitance, conductivity, rate capability, making the composite highly suitable for advanced Supercapacitor applications [75]. The high specific capacitance, excellent rate performance, and robust cycling stability observed in the results validate this compositional strategy.
The systematic diagram of the methods of synthesis of rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5 is shown in Fig. 1 .

The absorption spectrum of rGO@NCQDs was analyzed using a UV–Vis double beam spectrophotometer (Labman-LMSP-UV-1900), while emission spectra were obtained using a FLUOROMAX PLUS fluorometer (model 0597E-3122-FMPLUS R928P). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted using a Thermoscientific-Nicolet Summit LITE iD1 instrument. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a Rigaku Smartlab SE diffractometer. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) imaging was carried out using a Zeiss EVO 18 microscope, and Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) analysis was conducted using an AMETEK system. Electrochemical measurements were performed using a Biologic VSP-3e potentiostat.

The electrode slurry was prepared by mixing the as-prepared rGO@NCQDs/rGONCQDs@V2O5 with PVDF and carbon back in a ratio of 80:10:10, respectively, using NMP as the solvent. The mixture was then coated onto graphite foam and dried at 80 °C for 8 h. Prior to use, the graphite foam was washed sequentially with hydrochloric acid, double-distilled water, and acetone. The mass of the active material was determined by weighing the rectangular graphite piece before and after the coating process.

A 1 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte for the rGO@NCQDs system, while a 1 M KCl solution was used for the rGONCQDs@V2O5 system. The graphite foam coated with the prepared slurry serves as the working electrode, with silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) electrode used as the reference electrode for performance analysis in the three-electrode system

For the investigation of the electrode characteristics cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and capacity retention measurements were conducted. CV and GCD analysis were performed within a suitable potential window of −0.6 to 0.6 V, with scan rates ranging from 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s. For GCD analysis, current densities were varied from 1A/g to 10A/g.

Specific capacitance measurements were calculated based on the obtained results using the equation mentioned below [76].

In the specific capacitance calculation for cyclic voltammetry (CV), the numerator term represents the area under the CV curve, I denotes the current of the CV curve, k represents the scan rate, m is the mass (in grams) of the material, ΔV (in V) is the difference in the potential window range.

For galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) analysis, the specific capacitance was calculated using the results from non-linear GCD curves obtained at different current densities as mentioned above. The equation used for specific capacitance calculation during GCD analysis is as follows:

where, C denotes specific capacitance (in F/g), V represents the potential window (in V), and Δt is the discharge time (in hours) of the potential drop.

The fundamental behavior of the electrode material for supercapacitor applications was examined through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis. The impedance data were modeled using a well-known R-C equivalent circuit [77] to understand the electrical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties analysis and Solvatochromic measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of rGO@NCQDs play a crucial role in their potential applications, and thus their optical characteristics were thoroughly investigated.

In Fig. 2 (a), the plot of wavelength versus absorption depicts the absorption spectra of synthesized rGO@NCQDs in different solvents (ethyl acetate, acetonitrile, ethanol, and methanol) within the range of 250 nm to 500 nm. These absorption bands resemble those reported for NCQDs prepared using different methods [78,79], inactive of the π-π* transitions of the –C = C- bonds within the sp2 carbon core [80,81].

The absorption spectra of synthesized rGO@NCQDs in solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, and ethyl acetate) exhibit a bathochromic shift (shifting of absorption or emission spectrum of molecule towards longer wavelengths side. This shift suggests that the synthesized NPs/CQDs exhibit enhances light absorption capabilities compared to individual and absorb photons with lower energies, extending its optical absorption range into the visible or even near-infrared regions). Interestingly, this solvent effect showed an inverted Solvatochromic behavior, changing from positive to negative with increasing solvent polarity. This sensitivity enables tailored optical properties for diverse applications, highlighting the influence of solute-solvent interactions on rGO@NCQDs. In Fig. 2(d), the emission spectrum is depicted within the range of 325 nm to 650 nm. The absorption and emission peak values in different solvents are shown in Table 1 .

The effects of bathochromic shift of the spectrum have been reported by researchers into the near infrared region for its utilization as photoacoustic or photothermal agents in biomedical applications [82-85]. Another study is also available, the emphasis of which is to highlights bathochromic shift due to the solute-solvent interaction, affecting the fluorescence properties, and applications in sensors [86-87].

The optical energy band gap of rGO@NCQDs in various solvents was established by plotting (αhν)2 against photon energy (hν) [88]. Figs. 2(b) and 2(c) show plots for methanol and ethyl acetate, respectively, revealing energy band gaps varying between 2.98 eV to 3.86 eV for the selected solvents. This confirms the polarity-dependent function of the solute [89-90].

The estimation of the ground to excited state dipole moment ratio was conducted using the Solvatochromic shift method, as detailed in previous studies [91-95]. This method relies on systematic analyses performed by various researchers and involves establishing linear correlation equations using methods such as Biot-Kawski, Bakhshiev, Kawski-Chamma-Vallet, Lippert-Mataga, and Reichardt, along with microscopic solvent polarity parameters.

Biot-Kawski method correlates the absorption and emission maxima of a solute in different solvents to the solvent polarity function, particularly suitable for systems having both the dielectric constant and the refractive index of the solvents significantly influence the electronic transitions. Lippert-Mataga method and Bakshiev method correlates the Stokes shift to the solvent polarity function with the limitation that Lippert-Mataga method is sometimes less sensitive to subtle changes in solvent polarity compared to the Biot-Kawski method, and Bakhiev method includes additional terms to account for specific solute-solvent interactions, can be more complex and less straightforward than the Biot-Kawski method. Kawski-Chamma-Vallet method provides a modified approach to the Solvatochromic shift analysis, focusing on specific solute-solvent interactions with the requirement of more extensive data sets for reliable correlation. Reichardt method is based on empirical solvent polarity parameter to establish correlations with the limitations particularly for highly polar or non-polar solvent environments.

The spectroscopic results of this analysis are depicted in Fig. 3 (a) to 3(f) respectively. These plots illustrate the relationships between different methods and parameters dependent on the dielectric constant and refractive index of the solvent.

The results of different methods, including respective slopes, intercepts, and correlation coefficients, are presented in Table 2 . The correlation coefficient values obtained, which are greater than 0.97, indicating a strong linear relationship, reliable measurements, and the suitability of measuring the dipole moment with the respective solvents.

Although, other methods like Lippert-Mataga, Bikhshiev, Kawski-Chamma-Viallet are providing useful insights. However, Biot-Kawski methods indicating a strong linear relationship (0.9986 & 1.000), provide a more comprehensive analysis of solvent effects on the ground-to-excited state dipole moment ratio. The ratio of 4.60 of the single excited state to the ground state dipole moment calculated by Biot-Kawski method suggests a strong electronic transition in the synthesized materials influenced by the solvent polarity, with a higher degree of polarization in the single excited state compared to the ground state.

These results additionally confirm the predominance of carbon and oxygen by weight. Material mapping demonstrated the consistent dispersion of these identified elements throughout the synthesized material.

Additionally, SEM and EDX analyses conducted for rGONCQDs@V2O5 are presented in Fig. 4(c) and (d) respectively. The flower-shaped morphology observed, coupled with the presence of vanadium (V) alongside the previously detected element, confirm the formation of the composite material. The weight and atomic percentage of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O) and vanadium (V) were found to be 47.8 % and 64.7 %, 4.7 % and 4.6 %, 23.9 % and 22.9 %, 23.6 % and 7.8 % respectively. These results provide further evidence of the composition and confirm the presence of vanadium in the synthesized rGONCQDs@V2O5 composite.

The presence of carbonaceous materials in the synthesized composite, facilitates rapid electron transport during charging-discharging process, enhance the electrical conductivity, leading to improved electrochemical performance. Further, carbonaceous materials, having porous structure, are providing a high surface area for electrolyte ion adsorption. Thus enhances the accessibility of active sites and promotes efficient ion diffusion within the electrode material, crucial for achieving high capacitance and rapid charge storage in supercapacitors [96]. Further, the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the carbon lattice introduces additional Faradic reactions, contributing to the overall capacitance of the supercapacitor. This pseudocapacitive contribution enhances the energy density and specific capacitance of the device, allowing for higher charge storage capacity [97,98].

Oxygen functionalization on the surface of carbon based material, enhances the surface wettability, facilitates electrolyte ion adsorption, and promotes redox reaction at the electrode-electrolyte interface, thereby, improving the overall capacitance and performance of the supercapacitor. Vanadium ions present in the composite can undergo reversible redox reactions, contributing to the pseudocapacitive behaviour of the electrode material. The incorporation of the same introduces additional redox-active sites, enhancing the overall capacitance and energy storage capacity of the supercapacitor [99-104].
The FTIR spectra of the synthesized rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5 are depicted in Fig. 5 (a) and 5(b) respectively.

The surface functional groups in rGO-decorated NCQDs and its composite with V2O5 were investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). In the case of rGO-decorated NCQDs, the broad and overlapped strong band at 3490 cm−1 signifies stretching vibrations associated with OH and NH bonds. A vibrational band at 1272 cm−1 suggests the presence of CN groups on the rGO@NCQDs surface. Additionally, the appearance of a doublet at 2929 cm−1 and 2847 cm−1 along with a peak at 1652 cm−1 indicates the presence of aldehydic carbonyl groups. The band observed at 1589 cm−1 is attributed to NH vibration, indicating the presence of amino functional groups [105,106].

The presence of aldehydic group in rGO@NCQDs can have positive impact on the electrochemical properties by increasing charge storage capacity, enhancing redox activity, improving conductivity and providing sites for further functionalization. These effects contribute to the overall performance of the material making it suitable for various electrochemical applications especially in supercapacitors and batteries where redox reaction play crucial role [107-110].

The presence of an aromatic skeleton (sp2 hybridized carbon) in the rGO@NCQDs is confirmed by characteristics bending vibrational bands for CH and stretching vibration for C = C at 756 cm−1 and 1434 cm−1. Therefore, the FTIR analysis of rGO@NCQDs indicates the presence of various functional groups on the surface, including alcohols, amines, aromatic rings, and carbonyls, alongside sp2/sp3 core carbon derived from Moosa balbeesiaana peels.

The incorporation of V2O5 may cause shifts in the existing peaks in the FTIR spectrum of NCQDs; the observed shifts in the FTIR peaks upon the incorporation of V2O5 into the rGO@NCQDs composite suggest the occurrence of chemical interaction between the V2O5 species and the functional groups present in the carbon matrix. Common peaks of V = O and V–O-V can be observed at 935 and 618cm−1 respectively. These peaks indicate a strong interaction between V2O5 particles and rGO@NCQDs layers [111,112]. The shifts in peaks can also reflect change in the oxidation states of functional groups induced by the presence of V2O5 [113].

For instance a shift of characteristics bond at 935 cm−1 may be ascribed to the strong interaction between rGO@NCQDs and V2O5 [114]. The peaks positioned at 1208, 1560 and 3363 cm−1 are related to the CO, C = C, and OH, respectively. Thus due to the presence of mixed types of peaks of V2O5 and rGO@NCQDs, it can be concluded that the prepared material is rGO@NCQDs- V2O5 nanocomposites [115].

These shifts can be attributed to changes in bond lengths or strengths due to interactions between V2O5 and functional groups within rGO@NCQDs. The incorporation of V2O5 introduces new peaks and increases the intensity of existing peaks due to the formation of new chemical bonds.
Figs. 6 (a) and 6(b) show the XRD patterns illustrating the crystalline nature of the synthesized rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@ V2O5, respectively.

In the case of rGO@NCQDs, diffraction peaks were detected at 2θ values of 7.04°, 23.27°, 40.90°, and 46.05° For rGONCQDs@V2O5, the same were observed at 2θ values of 7.51°, 14.66°, 15.49°, 25.47°, 28.97°, 30.68°, 33.96°, 44.26°, 45.23°, and 49.48° The Debye-Scherrer equation [116] was used to calculate the particle sizes, resulting in average particle sizes of 0.49 nm for rGO@NCQDs and 6.79 nm for rGONCQDs@V2O5. Additional measured parameters are detailed in Table 3 .

The particle size significantly influences the electrochemical properties and suitability of materials for Supercapacitor applications [117,118]. Based on electrochemical analysis results, it can be predicted that the synthesized composite of rGO@NCQDs, with a size of 0.49 nm, is suitable for high-power applications due to high specific capacitance, rapid charge/discharge rates, high power density, making it ideal for applications requiring quick energy delivery. Conversely, the synthesized composite of rGONCQDs@ V2O5 with a size of 6.79 nm, is suitable for applications requiring high energy density and long-term cycling stability, making it excellent for high-performance energy storage with both high energy and power densities.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis typically measures the total charge stored in the electrode material over a wide range of potential windows and scan rates, providing information on the material's capacitive behavior across different voltage regimes. In contrast, Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) analysis measures the charge/discharge behavior at a constant current density, focusing on the material's charge storage capacity under specific operating conditions.

The electrochemical properties of rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5 were analyzed using cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves across different scan rates (from 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s) within a potential window range of −0.6 to 0.6 V in 1 M Na2SO4 and 1 M KCl aqueous electrolytes, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (a) and 8(a) respectively. The CV curves exhibit a quasi-rectangular shape without distinct redox peaks typically seen in pseudocapacitor, indicating electric double-layer and pseudocapacitive behavior of the synthesized composite materials. The symmetric profile of the Faradic peak and minimal peak separation at higher applied potential values suggest excellent reversibility and rapid charge transfer processes occurring on the surface of these green-synthesized and cost-effective materials [119]. Furthermore, the shift of oxidation peaks towards more positive directions and reduction peaks towards more negative directions with increasing scan rates indicates the polarization effect of the electrode [120].

Specific capacitance values were determined for rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5 at various scan rates, as illustrated in Fig. 7(b) and 8(b) respectively. For rGO@NCQDs, the specific capacitance values were measured at 599.40 F/g (10 mV/s), 428.1 F/g (20 mV/s), 347.74 F/g (30 mV/s), 264.51 F/g (50 mV/s), 209.77 F/g (75 mV/s), and 176.44 F/g (100 mV/s). Conversely, for rGONCQDs@V2O5, specific capacitance values were recorded at 1526.06 F/g (10 mV/s), 984.08 F/g (30 mV/s), 786.21 F/g (50 mV/s), 679.19 F/g (75 mV/s), and 577.97 F/g (100 mV/s). The observed gradual decrease in specific capacitance with increasing scan rate is attributed to charge diffusion during the charge storage mechanism [121-123]. This trend reflects the influence of scan rate on the kinetics of charge transfer and storage within the composite materials.

Figs. 7(c) and 8(c) depict the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves obtained at various current densities for rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5, respectively. The specific capacitance values gradually decrease due to the electrochemical diffusion process during the charge-discharge cycles with increasing current density.

For rGO@NCQDs (Fig. 7(f)), specific capacitance values were measured at 92.57 F/g, 46.58 F/g, 22.75 F/g, 13.66 F/g, 8.53 F/g, and 5.43 F/g corresponding to current densities of 1A/g, 2A/g, 4A/g, 6A/g, 8A/g, 10A/g respectively. The corresponding values of energy density values were calculated at 18.51 Wh/kg, 9.32 Wh/kg, 4.55 Wh/kg, 2.73 Wh/kg, 1.71 Wh/kg, and 1.09 Wh/kg, with power density values of 600 Wh/kg, 1200 Wh/kg, 2400 Wh/kg, 3600 Wh/kg, 4800 Wh/kg, and 6000 Wh/kg, as illustrated in Fig. 7(g).

Fig. 8 (f) illustrates the variation of specific capacitance with current density for rGONCQDs@ V2O5. Specific capacitance values were measured at 3005.99 F/g, 286.49 F/g, 186.12 F/g, 143.75 F/g, and 124.69 F/g corresponding to current densities of 1A/g, 2A/g, 3A/g, 4A/g, and 5A/g respectively. The corresponding values of energy density were calculated as 601.19 Wh/kg, 57.29 Wh/kg, 37.22 Wh/kg, 28.74 Wh/kg, and 24.93 Wh/kg, with power density values of 600 Wh/kg, 1299 Wh/kg, 1800 Wh/kg, 2400 Wh/kg, and 3000 Wh/kg, as shown in Fig. 8(g).The observed decrease in energy density with increasing current density, alongside the rise in power density, highlights the potential of these prepared materials for Supercapacitor applications. This behavior underscores their capability for efficient charge storage and rapid energy delivery, essential characteristics for high-performance energy storage devices.

To evaluate assess the electrochemical performance of the biosynthesized materials; a two-electrode symmetric system was utilized. In this setup, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) analyses were conducted using the prepared material as both the anode and cathode. The experimental parameters were consistent with those used in the three-electrode system, and the results are presented in Figs. 9 and 10 for rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@ V2O5, respectively.

Fig. 9(a) presents the cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis conducted across a scan rate range of 10 to 100 mV/s, where specific capacitance values were determined as follows: 210F/g (10 mV/s), 149F/g (20 mV/s), 122F/g (30 mV/s), 94F/g (50 mV/s), 76F/g (75 mV/s), and 65F/g (100 mV/s) (Fig. 9(b)). The corresponding galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves are depicted in Fig. 9(c), and the specific capacitance values are plotted in Fig. 9(d). Specific capacitance values at current densities of 1A/g, 2A/g, 3A/g, 4A/g, and 5A/g, were found to be 10.13F/g, 5.02F/g, 2.29 F/g, 0.95F/g, and 0.47F/g, respectively. Additionally, an energy density value of 2.03 Wh/kg was calculated at a power density of 600 W/kg for a current density of 1A/g in the energy density versus power density analysis.

Furthermore, specific capacitance values for rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5 were determined to be 562.62F/g, 312.07 F/g, 228.19 F/g, 182.20F/g, and 139.06 F/g at the same scan rates (Fig. 10(b)). In the GCD analysis, specific capacitance values were found to be 3012.20F/g, 288.49 F/g, 183.24 F/g, 141.69 F/g, and 40.26F/g across the same range of current densities. Additionally, an energy density value of 602.44 Wh/kg was calculated at a power density of 600 Wh/kg for a current density of 1A/g.

The cyclic stability of rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5 was assessed using galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) processes, as shown in Figs. 9(f) and 10(f). The analysis was conducted at a constant current density within the potential range of −0.6 V to +0.6 V over 5000 cycles.

For rGO@NCQDs, the capacitance retention was observed to be 100 % for the initial 1500 cycles, gradually decreasing to 95 % at 2000 cycles. This retention was maintained at 90 % for 4500 cycles and finally settled at 89 % after 5000 cycles.

In the case of rGONCQDs@ V2O5, the capacitance retention remained at 100 % for the first 2000 cycles, slightly decreasing to 99 % at 2500 cycles, and then stabilizing until 3000 cycles. Subsequently, the retention increased to 100 % at 3500 cycles and remained constant up to 5000 cycles.
This analysis demonstrates the favorable cyclic stability of both materials, with rGONCQDs@ V2O5 exhibiting particularly robust performance over the entire cycle range.

EIS analysis was conducted using Nyquist plot measurements for the three-electrode system to assess the conductive nature of the composite materials. Fig. 11 (a) and Fig. 11(b) present the EIS spectra of r-GO NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5, respectively, along with their equivalent Randles circuits.

In Fig. 11(a), the slanted straight line observed in the low-frequency range followed by a small semicircle at high frequency for r-GO NCQDs indicates a 'faradic' charge transfer redox reaction. The small diameter of the semicircle suggests fast electrochemical kinetics [124]. Conversely, the absence of a semicircle in the EIS spectrum of rGONCQDs@ V2O5 indicates slower electrochemical kinetics.

The slanted straight line observed in the lower frequency range of the electrochemical impedance spectrum signifies the non-faradic electrochemical double-layer charge storage mechanism at the electrode-electrode interface [125,126]. Additionally, the equivalent series resistances (ESR) were determined from the X-axis intercepts of the electrochemical impedance spectrum. These intercepts represent the resistance at the interface between the electrode and current collector, providing important insights into the overall impedance and performance characteristics of the electrochemical system.

For rGO@NCQDs (R(CR)(CR)O circuit), the corresponding values of resistance and capacitance were found to be 28.94 Ω, 5.6 × 10−6 F, 2.83 × 10−10 Ω, 0.0005462 F, and 0.0001411 Ω, respectively. For rGONCQDs@ V2O5 (R(CR)(CR)O circuit), the values were 1.397 Ω, 6.47 × 10−8 F, 0.0388 Ω, 0.0005444 F, and 0.2471 Ω, respectively [127]. These EIS results provide insights into the electrochemical behavior and interface characteristics of the synthesized composite materials.

Nitrogen-doped rGO-decorated NCQDs derived from Moosa balbeesiaana peels were successfully synthesized, achieving a significant ground to excited state dipole moment ratio of 4.6 using the Solvatochromic method. Optical property investigations revealed a varying energy band gap from 2.98 to 3.86 eV with solvent polarity changes, highlighting the tunability of these materials. The confirmation of rGO@NCQDs formation was supported by an average particle size of 0.49 nm.

The electrochemical performance of rGO@NCQDs and rGONCQDs@V2O5 composites was analyzed for supercapacitor applications through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) analysis, both in three-electrode and two-electrode systems. The results indicate excellent capacitance behavior of rGONCQDs@V2O5. Additionally, the ESR suggests efficient diffusion of electrolyte ions within the synthesized material.

Furthermore, cyclic stability analysis over 5000 cycles demonstrated outstanding capacitance retention of 100 %, underscoring the potential of these materials for long-term supercapacitor applications.
